[
  {
    "id": "apra-fleet-qv1",
    "title": "[bug][important] Parallel doer streak dispatch to a single member is unsafe -- causes hours-long hangs + response corruption; needs worktree-per-streak redesign",
    "description": "Auto-sprint's Develop phase can assign multiple doer streaks to run "in
parallel" within one round (e.g. `Develop C1 R1` on 2026-07-15 assigned
streaks `[["apra-fleet-fih.2"],["apra-fleet-fih.3"]]`). Today this caused a
live sprint (`apra-fleet-fih`) to stall for over an hour with zero forward
progress: both streaks were dispatched concurrently to the SAME fleet
member (`fleet-reorg`), which is a single working-directory/checkout, not
an isolated session per dispatch.

## Constraint (confirmed by user, authoritative)

A member = one folder/checkout. It is NOT possible to safely run two
concurrent `execute_prompt` dispatches against the same member -- there is
no isolation between them (shared git checkout, shared session/process).

The only correct way to achieve genuine parallel doer work is: register
multiple members, each pointed at its own git worktree, dispatch one
streak per member/worktree, and after all streaks complete, merge the
worktree branches back with proper branch hygiene (clean up worktrees,
resolve/avoid conflicts, delete merged branches). This is real
infrastructure work (worktree lifecycle + member registration automation +
post-merge cleanup), not a one-line fix.

## What happened without this (observed failure mode)

Two concurrent dispatches against one shared-folder member produced:
- Repeated 900s inactivity timeouts on both streaks (contention for the
  same folder/session -- likely git index lock or transport-level
  single-invocation assumption).
- One dispatch's response came back with a corrupted multi-byte UTF-8
  sequence (`📋` emoji prefix decoded as U+FFFD replacement character),
  breaking JSON schema-repair parsing. Plausibly caused by output
  interleaving between the two concurrent invocations against a channel
  the transport assumes is single-occupancy per member.
- One streak (`fih.2`) left a real, syntactically-valid but unverified
  partial edit in the working tree when its dispatch died mid-response --
  recovered and committed as a WIP checkpoint
  (`34f2752` on `test/apra-fleet-se-speedup`) rather than lost.

## Immediate stopgap (do before the real fix, cheap and safe)

Until worktree-per-streak isolation exists, `runner.js`'s Develop-phase
streak dispatch must not fan out more than one streak concurrently against
a single member. Either serialize streak dispatch (one at a time, same
member) or hard-fail pre-sprint validation if the streak-assignment output
would produce >1 streak with only one member available/registered for the
scope. Silent concurrent dispatch against one folder must not be possible
-- it should fail loud, not hang for an hour.

## Real fix (needs its own planning pass, do not attempt inline)

1. Member-per-worktree provisioning: given a scope with N ready parallel
   streaks, auto-create N git worktrees (or reuse pre-registered
   worktree-backed members) before Develop phase dispatch.
2. Route each streak's doer dispatch to its own worktree-backed member.
3. Post-round merge/branch-hygiene step: after all streaks in a round
   complete (success or failure), merge each worktree's branch back into
   the sprint branch, handle conflicts (fail loud + surface to reviewer,
   do not auto-resolve), then remove the now-merged worktrees so they don't
   accumulate across rounds/sprints.
4. Decide member registration lifecycle: are worktree-backed members
   ephemeral (registered/deregistered per sprint) or a fixed pool reused
   across sprints? Affects `~/.apra-fleet/data/registry.json` growth and
   whether stale worktree directories need periodic GC.
5. Update `doer.md`/`planner.md` prose contracts if the scope/member
   assignment model changes as part of this (may intersect with the
   graph-consistency remediation plan's Phase B/C work already tracked
   separately).

Acceptance criteria for this backlog item once planned: a sprint that
assigns 2+ parallel doer streaks completes them in genuinely isolated
worktrees, merges cleanly with no manual intervention required in the
common case, and any merge conflict is surfaced as a loud, actionable
failure rather than silent data loss or an hour-long hang.
",

  {
    "id": "apra-fleet-5fi",
    "title": "[feature] Inject role-prefixed bd memories (doer-memory-*, reviewer-memory-*, ...) into auto-sprint dispatches -- operator-extensible prompting without a submodule PR",
    "description": "## Problem

There is currently no way for a repo operator to superimpose extra prose
guidance onto a dispatched role (doer, reviewer, streak-assignment,
integ-test-runner, etc.) without editing that role's prompt contract file
directly (`vendor/apra-pm/agents/<role>.md`) -- which requires committing
inside the `vendor/apra-pm` git submodule, opening a PR there, merging it,
then bumping the submodule pointer in `apra-fleet-reorg` and rebuilding
`dist/agents/*.md` before the change takes effect. This is the correct,
heavyweight path for stable, designed contract changes -- but it is far too
slow for a quick, project-specific operator note (e.g. "on this machine,
always double-quote `bd list --status=` filters" or "never busy-wait poll a
backgrounded test command for more than 60s without emitting a status
line") that should take effect on the very next dispatch.

This session hit multiple real examples of exactly this kind of gotcha
(the PowerShell comma-array-mangling bug, the member-is-one-folder
parallel-dispatch constraint, the busy-wait/inactivity-timeout interaction)
-- each would have been directly prevented if a prior sprint's learning had
been immediately available to the next dispatch, with no rebuild cycle.

## Proposed mechanism

Reuse `bd`'s existing memory feature (`bd remember` / `bd memories`,
already used for cross-session operator knowledge -- see `bd prime`'s
"Persistent Memories" block) as the storage and retrieval layer, with a
role-prefixed key convention:

- `bd remember --key doer-memory-<slug> "..."`
- `bd remember --key reviewer-memory-<slug> "..."`
- `bd remember --key planner-memory-<slug> "..."`
- (one prefix per dispatched role/agentType that `runner.js` supports --
  enumerate all: planner, plan-reviewer, doer, reviewer, integ-test-runner,
  deployer, harvester)

Scope: **global**, stored in the project's own beads DB (not
sprint-specific, not bead/item-specific -- confirmed explicitly out of
scope by the repo owner). A memory written today is visible to every
future sprint run against this repo, indefinitely, until updated/removed.

## Integration point

In `packages/apra-fleet-se/auto-sprint/runner.js`, at each dispatch call
site (the same ones identified for the `max_total_s` ceiling work: Doer,
Reviewer x2, Integ-Test-Runner, plus Planner, Plan-Reviewer, Deployer,
Harvester for completeness), before building the dispatch prompt:

1. Run `bd memories <role>-memory --json` (e.g. `bd memories doer-memory
   --json`) to fetch any matching operator memories for that role.
2. If any are returned, append them to the prompt under a clearly-labeled,
   distinct section (e.g. `## Project-specific operator notes`) so the
   model understands this is supplementary, operator-authored guidance --
   not part of the base agent contract, and not to be confused with
   untrusted agent-output content (no `wrapUntrustedBlock` fencing needed;
   this is operator-authored, trusted content, same trust level as the
   rest of the dispatch prompt).
3. If no memories match, the section is omitted entirely -- no empty
   headers, no behavior change from today for a project that hasn't
   adopted this convention yet.

## Documentation requirement (explicit part of this bead's scope)

This is as important as the code: document the existence of this
extensibility point clearly, so operators actually discover and use it
instead of reaching for the heavyweight submodule-PR path out of habit.
At minimum:

- Add a section to `packages/apra-fleet-se/auto-sprint/docs/README.md`
  (the existing "how to run auto-sprint correctly" doc) explaining the
  `<role>-memory-<slug>` convention, with a concrete example
  (`bd remember --key doer-memory-powershell-quoting "..."`), and stating
  explicitly that this is global/repo-wide, takes effect on the next
  dispatch with no rebuild, and is the preferred path for
  operational/empirical gotchas vs. the submodule-PR path for stable
  contract design changes.
- Note the enumerated list of valid role prefixes (must match `runner.js`'s
  actual `agentType` values exactly, or a typo'd key silently never
  matches anything -- consider whether `runner.js` should also emit a
  one-line log/warning listing which memory keys (if any) were actually
  applied to a given dispatch, purely for operator visibility/debugging,
  not enforcement).

## Acceptance criteria

- A `bd remember --key doer-memory-<slug> "..."` memory is visibly
  included in the next Doer dispatch's prompt (verifiable via the sprint
  log's own dispatch-prompt narration, or a direct unit test against the
  prompt-building function).
- No matching memories -> no section appended, no error, no change from
  current behavior.
- All roles enumerated above are covered, not just doer/reviewer.
- `packages/apra-fleet-se/auto-sprint/docs/README.md` documents the
  convention with a working example.
- Existing `advanced-mock-runner-test.mjs`/split successor tests still
  pass unmodified (this is additive prompt content, not a change to
  dispatch control flow) -- add new test coverage for the injection
  itself rather than relying on existing tests to incidentally catch it.

## Explicitly out of scope for this bead

- Sprint-specific or bead/item-specific memory scoping (e.g.
  `doer-memory-<bead-id>-<slug>`) -- confirmed not needed right now.
- Any UI/dashboard surface for authoring memories -- `bd remember` on the
  CLI is sufficient.
- Automatic memory *authoring* by the harvester or reviewer roles (a
  separate, related idea discussed the same session -- track as its own
  follow-up bead if pursued, do not conflate with this one, which is only
  about the *retrieval/injection* mechanism for memories a human already
  wrote).
",

    "description": "packages/apra-fleet-se/bin/cli.mjs main(): the try/catch/finally around engine.executeFile() (~lines 579-597) unconditionally runs server.close(); transport.stop(); process.exit() in finally for ANY escaping exception, killing the dashboard (port 18300) even when the sprint merely FAILED legitimately.

Files to change:
- packages/apra-fleet-se/bin/cli.mjs (main() try/catch/finally around executeFile only).

Expected behaviour:
- Separate 'the sprint run has ended (pass or fail)' from 'the CLI process/dashboard should exit'.
- On a caught sprint-level failure, keep the dashboard HTTP server + transport ALIVE and update its displayed state to FAILED/ABORTED so the user can inspect the outcome; do NOT call server.close()/transport.stop()/process.exit() in the finally block for that case.
- Only tear down + exit on explicit user action (Stop control) or after an idle/inactivity window post-failure.
- Preserve process.exitCode semantics so external callers still observe failure.

ACCEPTANCE:
- A sprint-level exception no longer synchronously closes the dashboard server; port 18300 stays reachable and shows FAILED/ABORTED.
- Successful runs still terminate as before.
- ASCII only.",

    "id": "apra-fleet-xbu.2",
    "title": "[bug] auto-sprint has no recovery path for a Planner dispatch interrupted mid-DAG-authoring",
    "description": "PARTIALLY FIXED -- flagged for review 2026-07-16. Independently verified against current packages/apra-fleet-se/auto-sprint/runner.js.

Original claim (2026-07-15): Pre-sprint validation hard-fails on 'zero ready beads in scope' with no distinction between 'sprint genuinely complete' and 'stale cyclic DAG left by an interrupted prior planning pass' -- every relaunch just hard-fails at the same gate, with no self-heal path. Suggested fix: teach pre-sprint validation to detect a cycle specifically (vs. genuinely-zero work), and either auto-repair the common 2-node parent+blocks case, or route into a Planner delta-repair pass instead of throwing.

Current state (verified 2026-07-16): the DETECTION half is now implemented (runner.js:1530-1550) -- it specifically scans not-done beads for a 'blocks' dependency that is also a parent-child ancestor/descendant pair, and if found, throws a distinctly-worded error naming the exact offending bead pairs AND the exact 'bd dep remove <a> <b>' fix command(s) needed, instead of the old generic 'no ready beads' message. The genuinely-empty-scope case (no cycle found) still gets a separate, correctly-distinguished diagnostic listing each not-done bead's blockers (runner.js:1552-1561).

REMAINING GAP: the suggested fix offered two options -- 'auto-repair the common 2-node case' OR 'route into a Planner delta-repair pass' -- and NEITHER is implemented yet. The current code only detects and reports the cycle with the fix command as guidance; it still throws and stops the sprint, requiring a human/operator to manually run the suggested 'bd dep remove' command and relaunch. No auto-repair, no automatic delta-replan dispatch. This remaining half of the original ask is still open and should be the sprint's actual scope for this bead (the detection/distinction problem is already solved).",

    "id": "apra-fleet-02s",
    "title": "[bug] execute_prompt timeouts bypass retry entirely; runner.js conflates dispatch vs schema errors; schema-repair must resume session instead of re-sending full context (wastes tokens)",
    "description": "TRIAGED 2026-07-16: all three findings verified still present in current source; only line numbers have drifted (unrelated commits added lines above these regions since filing).

Follow-up investigation (originally 2026-07-15) after diagnosing apra-fleet-j6i's Final Review timeout/FAIL. THREE distinct, confirmed findings: two are bugs (server-side retry-bypass, client-side error-type conflation), one is a decided fix (schema-repair should use session resume, not fresh self-contained re-asks -- confirmed by user 2026-07-15: "must use resume, else we are wasting tokens").

1) SERVER-SIDE BUG (execute-prompt.ts) -- CONFIRMED, line unchanged: the outer catch block (now verified at execute-prompt.ts:506-508) still sets structuredContent:{isError:true, reason:'dispatch_failed'} for a genuine command timeout/thrown exception, structurally separate from and unreachable from the two retry mechanisms in the preceding try block (stale-session retry, server/overloaded-error retry) -- both of which only fire on a non-throwing nonzero exit, never on a thrown exception. A genuine timeout throws, so it reaches neither retry path -- single-shot, unconditionally-unretried, for every dispatch site system-wide.

2) CLIENT-SIDE BUG (runner.js) -- CONFIRMED, now 6 combined-catch sites (was cited as "at minimum Final Review's, ~line 2201"; verified 2026-07-16 the same `err instanceof AgentOutputError || err instanceof AgentDispatchError` combined-branch pattern exists at runner.js:1249, 1740, 2161, 2218, 2398 (Final Review, was ~2201), and 2462). packages/apra-fleet-workflow/src/workflow/index.mjs still correctly types AgentDispatchError vs AgentOutputError distinctly (confirmed: index.mjs's doc comments and throw sites unchanged in intent), but runner.js's catch blocks still discard that distinction, reporting both as "schema-repair exhausted, treating as FAIL" regardless of which actually occurred.

3) DECIDED FIX -- schema-repair must use session resume, not a fresh self-contained re-ask (packages/apra-fleet-workflow/src/workflow/index.mjs) -- CONFIRMED, lines verified: repair loop at index.mjs:720 (`for (let attempt = 0; attempt <= maxRepairs; attempt++)`), `isRepair` computed at :728, still dispatches with a STATIC `resume: opts.resume ?? false` at :761 regardless of isRepair. The "F10" self-contained-re-ask rationale comment is still present (now ~:759-761 region). maxRepairs itself computed at :625 (`opts.schemaRetries ?? 2`).

SUGGESTED FIX (full scope, unchanged from original investigation): (1) execute-prompt.ts's outer catch (dispatch exception path) should have its own bounded retry (at minimum one retry with a fresh session, mirroring the existing stale-session-retry pattern) rather than being an unconditional single-shot failure. (2) runner.js's 6 combined-catch sites should branch on the error TYPE (AgentDispatchError vs AgentOutputError) and handle each distinctly -- an AgentDispatchError should trigger a dispatch-retry or a clearly-labeled "infrastructure failure, not a quality failure" outcome, never be conflated with schema-repair exhaustion. (3) switch index.mjs's schema-repair loop to force resume:true when isRepair===true and shrink buildRepairPrompt() to a lean reminder-only message (validation errors + "please respond again with corrected JSON only" -- not re-embedding the full original prompt/invalid output) -- verify session continuity holds specifically across repair rounds before shipping.",

    "id": "apra-fleet-202",
    "title": "Viewer/workflow: cost always $0 -- _resolveCost/calculateCost read wrong usage field names (prompt_tokens/completion_tokens vs input_tokens/output_tokens)",
    "description": "Confirmed live via /state API (2026-07-13): every agent activity shows real, non-null token usage (e.g. {input_tokens:43, output_tokens:11797, total_tokens:11840}) but cost is always exactly 0 (not null/unknown -- unknownCostCount stays 0 too), even though model tier ('premium') and token counts now render correctly in the UI (today's earlier fixes for 7b0/13o/0ak worked for those).

Root cause: packages/apra-fleet-workflow/src/workflow/index.mjs _resolveCost() (lines 563-582, added in d9852d7 'consume get_member_model_pricing for real per-member cost') reads usage.prompt_tokens / usage.completion_tokens. The ACTUAL usage object shape produced by execute-prompt.ts's structuredContent (and confirmed live in /state) is {input_tokens, output_tokens, total_tokens} -- different field names entirely. Both reads silently default to 0 via '|| 0', so cost always computes to exactly (0/1e6)*price + (0/1e6)*price = 0, a valid-looking number rather than null, so it's never counted as 'unknown'.

Same bug exists in the fallback path: packages/apra-fleet-workflow/src/workflow/pricing.mjs calculateCost() line 67 also checks/reads usage.prompt_tokens / usage.completion_tokens.

Not a missing feature -- this is a straightforward field-name mismatch. Likely pre-existing since d9852d7 but previously unreachable/masked because usage was always null before today's 7b0/13o fix started threading real usage through -- now that real usage flows in, this bug is actually exercised on every single agent call.

Fix: change both _resolveCost() and calculateCost() to read usage.input_tokens / usage.output_tokens (matching the actual shape), or normalize the usage object's field names once at the point execute-prompt.ts's structuredContent is consumed so both consumers don't need duplicate awareness of the shape.",

    "id": "apra-fleet-7lk",
    "title": "Viewer: activity tree squishes/compresses instead of scrolling -- missing flex-shrink:0 on .tree-group",
    "description": "Confirmed live via browser DOM testing (2026-07-13, cloneNode stress test on the real .tree-group/.group-body/.phase-body structure inside #stream-list).

packages/apra-fleet-workflow/src/viewer/index.mjs: #stream-list is 'display:flex; flex-direction:column; flex:1; overflow-y:auto' (correctly bounded per the apra-fleet-m0c fix). But its direct children, .tree-group (line ~41) -- and by extension .group-body (line 46) and .tree-phase/.phase-body (lines 49/54) nested inside them -- never set flex-shrink:0. Default flex-shrink:1 means once total content height exceeds #stream-list's bounded clientHeight, flexbox proportionally SHRINKS all .tree-group items to fit rather than letting them keep natural height and overflow -- so the scrollbar never actually engages and content is visually compressed/squished instead.

Reproduced: appended 45 additional clones of the real (open) .tree-group nodes into a live #stream-list and measured scrollHeight === clientHeight even with 48 groups present (would be ~1700-1900px of natural content vs a 1119px container) -- conclusive proof of shrink-to-fit rather than scroll-to-overflow. Matches the user's exact reported symptom: 'only way to view items is my collapsing some, while the expectation is that user must be able to scroll within the client area.'

This is a DIFFERENT, additional root cause from apra-fleet-m0c/5lj (already fixed, real but insufficient on its own -- that fix made #stream-list's overflow:auto correctly bounded, this bug is why it still never triggers in practice).

Fix: add flex-shrink: 0 to .tree-group (and verify .tree-phase / individual details.event-activity if they're also direct flex children needing the same treatment).",

    "id": "apra-fleet-7pm",
    "title": "[EPIC] apra-fleet workflow subsystem: SEA-binary workflow runner (import-trampoline launcher, on-disk runtime tree, built-in workflows)",
    "description": "Ship 'apra-fleet workflow <name>' as a new SEA-binary subcommand that runs workflow scripts (auto-sprint, hello-world, and future user-authored workflows) with ZERO system Node required. Full design: docs/workflow-subsystem-plan.md (this repo, feat/fleet-reorg).

Architecture decision (fixed -- do not relitigate in child tasks; see plan Section 1): "import trampoline + on-disk runtime tree" (option c). The SEA bundle gains a tiny launcher (src/cli/workflow.ts) that resolves the workflow under ~/.apra-fleet/workflows/<name>/, sets APRA_FLEET_SERVER_BIN / APRA_FLEET_SE_SCHEMAS_DIR only when unset, rewrites process.argv, and does an in-process dynamic import() of the entry file. The installer populates ~/.apra-fleet/node_modules/ with verbatim copies of @apralabs/apra-fleet-workflow, @apralabs/apra-fleet-client, and vendored ajv (+4 deps) so Node's normal upward node_modules walk resolves bare specifiers with NO loader hooks and NO dual-copy of error classes (avoids the instanceof-breakage hazard called out as the decisive constraint in Section 1). sea-bundle.cjs itself contains ZERO workflow-engine code.

Hard invariant: 'apra-fleet workflow <name>' is ALWAYS a separate client process from the apra-fleet MCP server it talks to (Section 1). The only open question is HOW the client reaches the server -- self-spawned stdio vs. attaching to an already-running HTTP singleton -- see R13 (a dedicated child task in this epic resolves it; do not merge launcher and server processes).

Known predecessor bead: apra-fleet-3ns.6 ('SEA binary: embed new auto-sprint as SEA assets', open, P3) asked exactly the question this plan answers (extraction destination, server resolution via APRA_FLEET_SERVER_BIN). This epic supersedes/absorbs it -- do not create duplicate parallel scope; 3ns.6 should be closed by whoever executes this epic's Phase 1 tasks, referencing this epic in the close reason. Left open (not closed) by this planning pass per planning-agent rules (never close issues while planning).

File-level collision note (Section 0): apra-fleet-dv5's children touch packages/apra-fleet-se/auto-sprint/runner.js and pricing.mjs. This epic's Phase 1 install-payload tasks install verbatim copies of those same files -- no rework required either way, but avoid two concurrent sprints editing runner.js at the same time.

Scope: docs/workflow-subsystem-plan.md Sections 1-10 in full (architecture, file/asset inventory, CLI surface, hello-world authoring contract, schema installation fix, install/uninstall/update integration, build pipeline changes, CI verification, risk register, phased task breakdown). Out of scope: merging the launcher and MCP server into one process (explicitly rejected in Section 1); anything under vendor/apra-pm/** unrelated to schemas; publishing @apralabs/apra-fleet-workflow/-client to npm standalone.

Done looks like: all Section 10 phases (1-4) complete; 'apra-fleet workflow hello-world' runs with zero system Node on all 3 OS in CI; auto-sprint is runnable as a built-in workflow via the launcher; docs/authoring-workflows.md exists; existing command surface (install/uninstall/run/--version) unchanged per the Phase 4 regression guard.",

    "title": "[EPIC] SaaS bootstrap/sync integration (apra-fleet.exe <-> fleet-dashboard) + hub-service retirement",
    "description": "Follow-on to the hub-spoke migration (apra-fleet-us9, closed) and the API contract reconciliation (docs/api-contract-reconciliation.md). Product-owner directive (2026-07-05): fleet-dashboard is the ONLY true persistence layer for workspace/project/member/secret configuration; apra-fleet.exe must never reimplement that persistence, even for a self-hosted story. Cross-checked against fleet-dashboard's own docs/architecture.md, which confirms this as their real, tested architecture (DynamoDB-backed, OAuth+session-cookie auth already built, iron-wall workspace isolation already tested).

User's explicit scoping decision (2026-07-05), reconciling this with today's working product: (1) today's standalone apra-fleet.exe (local registry.json, LocalStrategy, RemoteStrategy/SSH) continues to work exactly as-is, zero regression, SSH is NOT deprecated or gated behind anything -- it remains a permanent first-class execution transport, not a migration target. (2) The cloud/SaaS value-add is specifically: (a) easy bootstrap of workspaces/members/projects via a web UI instead of local-only CLI setup, and (b) reliable, durable storage of that configuration off fragile developer devices -- NOT replacing SSH-based execution with a relay. (3) src/hub-service/ (the Postgres-backed hub built this session) is retired to REFERENCE-ONLY status -- valuable as a working proof of the wire-protocol/security-isolation semantics, but fleet-dashboard is the real production tier-3 implementation going forward; hub-service is not deployed or extended further.     "title": "[test] installer installs a portable, working dolt CLI and degrades gracefully",
    "description": "Add vitest coverage (npm test / vitest, matching the existing src/cli test conventions) for the dolt install step end-to-end.

Files to change (<=2):
- test file for dolt-install (new, alongside the existing install tests)
- small fixture archive if needed

What to test / how to assert:
1. Asset resolution: resolveDoltAsset returns windows-amd64.zip for win32/x64, linux-amd64.tar.gz for linux/x64, the darwin arm64 asset for darwin/arm64; throws on an unsupported combo. Assert on the returned URL/filename string.
2. Download+extract with a stubbed fetch serving a fixture archive -> an executable dolt lands in a tmp destDir; assert fs.existsSync + the returned path, and (POSIX) the exec bit.
3. Graceful degradation: stub fetch to reject -> the install step warns and does NOT throw; assert the process/summary reports 'not available' and the overall install still resolves.
4. Idempotence: with a dolt already present in destDir, the install step performs zero fetch calls (assert the stub's call count is 0).
5. Step-counter integrity: assert the printed [n/total] labels are contiguous with the dolt step added.
6. Cleanup: after verifyDolt on a failure path, assert the scratch data dir no longer exists.

The real 'dolt sql-server' startup path (per apra-fleet-ire.2) is network/binary-dependent -- gate that assertion behind an opt-in env flag or skip-if-dolt-absent guard so CI stays hermetic and green on Windows.

Acceptance criteria:
- All the above assertions pass under 'npm test' on Windows and Linux.
- No test downloads from the real network by default; no test leaves a tmp dir or dolt process behind.
- Tests fail (not error/hang) if the install step ever becomes fatal on dolt failure.",

    "id": "apra-fleet-ire",
    "title": "Fleet installer: install and verify a portable dolt CLI alongside bd",
    "description": "src/cli/install.ts (~line 1040, the 'Beads install step') currently installs only @beads/bd via npm global install, with no dolt binary install. PoC (2026-07-16, dolt-poc-v2) confirmed that having a real dolt binary available (portable download, no admin/system install needed -- dolt v2.2.0 windows-amd64.zip, ~40MB download, single ~123MB static exe, no dependencies) and spinning up a temporary 'dolt sql-server' pointed directly at bd's existing '.beads/embeddeddolt' data dir (no migration needed) unlocks the documented dolt_conflicts / dolt_conflicts_resolve resolve-in-place path for Dolt merge conflicts -- which is otherwise completely blocked in bd's embedded mode ('bd sql'/'bd doctor' both hard-refuse). Measured footprint: ~73MB idle RSS, ~1.6s startup, single configurable port (default 3306), no system-wide footprint -- cheap enough to install unconditionally and spin up on-demand only when a conflict is actually detected (no persistent daemon required). Add a fleet-installer step (mirroring the existing Beads install step's already-installed-check pattern) that downloads/extracts the dolt binary to a per-install location (e.g. alongside the existing BIN_DIR, not system PATH) and verifies it via 'dolt version', then smoke-tests that a real 'dolt sql-server' can start against a scratch data dir and accept a connection. See the auto-sprint-service design plan (https://claude.ai/code/artifact/413dd5a5-68f1-4a46-b10f-992dff241d3f, Part 3.4) for the full verified PoC findings and the exact conflict-resolution command ritual this unlocks.",

  {
    "id": "apra-fleet-nh1",
    "title": "[bug] Dashboard outer Activity panel cannot scroll past initial viewport once expanded (body/panel overflow-y:hidden)",
    "description": "Live-verified 2026-07-15 via browser automation against the running apra-fleet-7pm dashboard (viewer/index.mjs served UI, port 18300). User reported: 'in the current fully expanded state there is no way to see anything beyond first page.'

Root cause confirmed via getComputedStyle(): both <body> and the active .tab-content.panel element have overflow-y: hidden (not auto/scroll). Their height is being set to exactly match content height (no viewport cap), so in THIS automated browser (which auto-grows its window to fit page content) no clipping was directly observable -- html/body/panel scrollHeight all equal their own clientHeight, i.e. the measurement environment masks the bug by growing the whole window instead of clipping. But on a real, fixed-size browser window smaller than the fully-expanded Activity tree's rendered height, overflow-y:hidden on both body and panel means there is NO scrollable container and NO scrollbar anywhere in the ancestor chain -- content below the fold is simply unreachable, matching the user's exact complaint.

This looks like a partial regression/incomplete fix of the earlier apra-fleet-m0c/5lj scroll bug (handoff doc: '.tab-content.active { display: block } silently overrode .panel's display:flex, breaking the nested flex/overflow scroll chain', fixed in packages/apra-fleet-workflow/src/viewer/index.mjs). The display:flex fix is confirmably in place now (panel computed display is 'flex', not overridden), but overflow-y on the scrollable container was left as hidden instead of being set to auto/scroll -- so the flex layout is correct but nothing was ever made scrollable.

Suggested fix: identify the intended scrollable pane (likely .panel or a nested .activity-list container) and set its CSS to overflow-y: auto with either a fixed/max height (e.g. calc(100vh - <header height>)) or flex: 1 1 auto within a height-constrained flex ancestor, so content beyond the viewport gets its own internal scrollbar instead of being clipped or requiring the whole window to grow.",

    "id": "apra-fleet-wrc",
    "title": "auto-sprint: permanently-failed doer streaks still get sent into Review with no actual work to review",
    "description": "Confirmed live during apra-fleet-7pm sprint (2026-07-14): Develop C1 R2 had both streaks ([apra-fleet-7pm.5] and [apra-fleet-7pm.7]) exhaust their retry-once and end with outcome:'failed' (both dispatch attempts hit the 300s inactivity timeout -- see apra-fleet-aw8). Log: 'Develop C1 R2 streak outcomes: [{beadIds:[apra-fleet-7pm.5],outcome:failed},{beadIds:[apra-fleet-7pm.7],outcome:failed}]', immediately followed by '--- Phase: Review C1 R2 ---' dispatching against those same bead ids.

Root cause: runner.js:1802 assignedBeadIds = streakOutcomes.flatMap((o) => o.beadIds) has no filter on o.outcome -- every streak's bead ids feed into the Review evidence-gathering (bd show + reviewer dispatch) regardless of whether the streak actually succeeded, failed with a real doer report, or (this case) failed with NO doer ever having successfully run at all.

Consequence: the Reviewer (premium tier, per FIXED_ROLE_TIER.reviewer) gets dispatched to 'review the work just done' for beads that have zero actual new work -- no commits, no diff, nothing changed from before the round started. Best case this wastes a premium-tier review call producing a CHANGES_NEEDED verdict that just re-states 'nothing was done' (functionally a no-op, just an expensive one); worst case it produces a confusing/incorrect verdict since the reviewer's prompt and contract assume real work happened.

Recommendation: filter streakOutcomes to only include outcome !== 'failed' bead ids in assignedBeadIds sent to Review; permanently-failed streaks' beads should just remain in their current (still-ready, never-closed) state and naturally get picked up again by the next Develop round's 'bd list --ready' scan -- no review dispatch needed for beads nothing happened to. Related to apra-fleet-aw8 (root cause of WHY streaks fail) but distinct -- this is about what the orchestrator does AFTER a streak is already known to have failed with no work product.",

  {
    "id": "apra-fleet-aw8",
    "title": "auto-sprint: doer-dispatch retry after inactivity timeout should escalate timeout + carry forward killed-attempt context (not blind resume)",
    "description": "Distilled from live analysis during the apra-fleet-7pm sprint run (2026-07-13), triggered by a real timeout+retry observed live on streak apra-fleet-7pm.3 ('Command timed out after 300000ms of inactivity' -> retried once -> succeeded).

CURRENT STATE:
- runner.js:1730-1750 dispatchDoer() retry: on failure, calls the IDENTICAL dispatchDoer() a second time -- same prompt, same (default) timeout_s, no resume flag override. No escalation of any kind.
- execute-prompt.ts:164/353: timeout_s defaults to a flat 300s (5 min) applied uniformly across every role -- planner/reviewer analysis calls AND doer streaks doing full impl+test+commit work share the same default.
- Confirmed already-solid: strategy.ts:112-118 does a proper tree-kill (killTree() before child.kill('SIGKILL')) on inactivity timeout before the retry starts -- no orphan-process/PID-collision risk from this mechanism (this was the concern behind the historical apra-fleet-kwx incident; already handled correctly here).
- Confirmed via code: retry does NOT use session resume. packages/apra-fleet-workflow/src/workflow/index.mjs:761 explicitly defaults resume: opts.resume ?? false (F10 / apra-fleet-unw.3, deliberate -- keeps workflow-authored prompts self-contained/deterministic), and dispatchDoer() never overrides opts.resume, so every retry is a fresh session with zero memory of the killed attempt; all partial work from the killed attempt is silently discarded and fully redone.

GAPS / RECOMMENDATIONS (user-reviewed 2026-07-13):
1. Escalate timeout_s on retry, linearly, e.g. 300s -> 600s -> 900s (5/10/15 min) rather than reusing the identical value on every attempt. Stay in timeout_s (seconds) throughout -- do NOT introduce a raw-ms public option; the codebase's existing public-facing config surface (timeout_s) is already seconds-based and should stay that way (internal strategy.ts setTimeout calls are fine in ms, that's Node's native timer API, not a public knob).
2. Role-specific default timeout_s: doer streaks run full impl+test+commit cycles, categorically heavier than planner/reviewer analysis calls, but share the same global 300s default. Consider a higher default specifically for doer-role dispatches (e.g. 900s).
3. DO NOT simply flip retries to resume:true. The killed attempt was hard SIGKILL'd mid-work (tree-killed after inactivity), not gracefully stopped -- its working directory / git state could be genuinely inconsistent (mid-file-edit, half-finished git operation). Blindly resuming a session whose process was just hard-killed risks the resumed conversation's understanding diverging from actual on-disk state -- this is the same risk category flagged in this project's own git-loss-incident-2026-07-02 memory ('timeout != process death', orphaned/half-finished git operations from overlapping dispatch). SAFER alternative that still achieves 'continue where we left off': have the retry prompt explicitly embed a git status/git diff snapshot of what the killed attempt left behind, so the fresh (self-contained, resume:false) session can see and account for partial work -- consistent with the existing design pattern already used for schema-repair re-asks (buildRepairPrompt in index.mjs embeds prior context directly in prompt text rather than relying on resume:true).
4. No telemetry/visibility today for 'timed out twice in a row' vs any other unrecoverable dispatch error -- both collapse into the same generic dispatchError -> streak marked 'failed' path (runner.js:1756-1766). If the SAME failure reason (inactivity timeout specifically) fires on both the original and retry, that's a strong signal the timeout is mis-sized for this task class, but nothing currently tags/surfaces that distinctly, making it hard to tell 'timeout needs tuning' from 'genuinely broken task' without reading raw logs.

Separate, smaller open question raised alongside this: should the rarely-used opts.timeoutMs (client-side MCP request-timeout override, AgentOptions field, distinct from the inactivity timeout_s) also be converted from ms to seconds for consistency? Not yet decided -- flagged for whoever picks this up to ask/decide, low priority, does not block items 1-4 above.",
    "notes": "SCOPE BROADENED (2026-07-13, confirmed live): the same no-escalation gap applies beyond doer streaks. dispatchReview() (runner.js:1134-1188) has its OWN 2-attempt retry loop with a smart contract-violation check (a CHANGES_NEEDED verdict with empty reopenIds AND empty newTasks is recognized as meaningless and auto-retried, throwing ReviewerContractViolationError only if it repeats) -- this is better-designed than the doer path, observed live working correctly after a real 300s inactivity timeout on the Reviewer. But its retry (runner.js:1139) reuses the identical default timeout_s with no escalation, same gap as the doer path. plan-reviewer (~runner.js:1513) and Final Review (~runner.js:2113) likely share the same pattern -- not yet individually confirmed. Recommend the timeout_s escalation fix (item 1 in this issue) be applied at a shared/common dispatch-retry layer if one gets introduced, rather than doer-only, so all 4 retry sites (doer, reviewer, plan-reviewer, final-review) benefit uniformly.",

  {
    "id": "apra-fleet-92d",
    "title": "auto-sprint: Streak Assignment mis-costed at premium tier; long-term should be deterministic JS, not an LLM call",
    "description": "Distilled from live analysis during the apra-fleet-7pm sprint run (2026-07-13).

CURRENT STATE: packages/apra-fleet-se/auto-sprint/runner.js:1627-1636 dispatches a dedicated LLM call ('Streak Assignment') every Develop round (up to 3x per cycle) to group ready beads into parallel-safe work streaks. It reuses agentType:'planner' and FIXED_ROLE_TIER.planner = 'premium' (runner.js:94-101) purely by code-reuse convenience, not because the task needs premium reasoning.

WHY IT'S SEPARATE FROM THE INITIAL PLANNER CALL (cannot simply be merged): the initial planner call builds the DAG once per Plan phase; Streak Assignment runs once per Develop round against whatever beads are CURRENTLY ready (bd list --ready), which changes as prior-round work completes -- that input doesn't exist at initial-planning time. Temporally separate steps, not mergeable into one call.

WHY IT COULD IN PRINCIPLE BE PURE JS (no LLM): bd's own dependency graph ('blocks' edges) already guarantees ordering-safety for the ready set -- a bead only becomes ready once its blockers close, so ready beads are already DAG-safe to run in any order/parallel from a pure-ordering standpoint. What Streak Assignment actually decides beyond that is FILE-COLLISION risk between formally-independent ready beads. That's a deterministic set-overlap (union-find) problem IF beads carried structured file-touch metadata.

WHY IT CAN'T BE PURE JS TODAY: no structured/validated file-touch field exists on beads. 11 of 13 tasks in this exact epic (apra-fleet-7pm) happen to include a free-text 'Files: ...' line in their prose description, but 2 (.7, .11) don't, and grep of vendor/apra-pm/agents/planner.md shows ZERO mentions of any 'Files:' convention -- it's not documented, schema-validated, or guaranteed present/well-formed. A JS parser scraping this would be brittle (though would degrade safely to 'own streak' for tasks missing the line, not corrupt anything).

TWO-PART RECOMMENDATION:
1. Quick low-risk win: add a distinct FIXED_ROLE_TIER['streak-assignment'] = 'standard' (or 'cheap') instead of reusing .planner's 'premium' tier. selectStreaks() (runner.js:606) already has a robust, always-correct fallback (one-bead-per-streak) whenever LLM output is invalid, so correctness isn't gated on premium-quality output -- low risk to downgrade.
2. Real structural fix: have the planner agent emit a validated 'touches: [file paths]' field as real bead metadata (not prose) when creating each task. Once that exists, Streak Assignment becomes pure deterministic JS (union-find over file-set intersection) -- eliminates the LLM call entirely (not just cheapens it), zero latency, more reliable than an LLM guess. Larger change: touches planner.md's task-authoring contract, beads schema/metadata, and runner.js's streak-assignment code path.",

    "id": "apra-fleet-0pu.1",
    "title": "[impl] Log an explicit 'Develop/Review round cap reached' message at loop exit",
    "description": "In the auto-sprint runner (packages/apra-fleet-se/auto-sprint/runner.js, the per-cycle 'while (devRounds < 3)' Develop/Review loop, ~lines 1689 and 1959-1964), add an explicit log line that fires when the loop exits because the round cap was hit with work still open -- distinct from the organic 'stillOpen.length === 0' completion.

Current bug (apra-fleet-0pu): when devRounds reaches 3 with beads still open/reopened, the loop logs the misleading 'System found N beads still open/ready. Looping back to develop.' and then silently exits into Deploy on the next while-condition check -- the 'Looping back' message implies the opposite of what actually happens.

Files to change: packages/apra-fleet-se/auto-sprint/runner.js (single file).

Suggested fix: after the while loop exits, detect the case devRounds === 3 AND the last iteration's stillOpen/currentReady was non-empty, and emit an explicit line such as: 'Develop/Review round cap (3) reached this cycle with N bead(s) still open/reopened -- deferring to next cycle.' Keep the organic 'stillOpen.length === 0' break path logging a distinct 'cycle organically complete' message. Do not change control flow or the carry-forward behavior (reopened beads already correctly flow to the next Sprint Cycle) -- this is a logging/observability fix only.

Acceptance criteria:
- Round-cap exit (devRounds===3 with work still open) logs a distinct, accurate 'round cap reached ... deferring to next cycle' line naming the count of still-open beads.
- The organic completion path (stillOpen empty) logs a clearly different message and no longer prints the misleading 'Looping back to develop' when the loop is actually terminating.
- No change to which beads carry into the next cycle; only log output changes.
- Existing runner behavior/tests unaffected.",

  {
    "id": "apra-fleet-9ub",
    "title": "Auto-sprint: add an explicit pause/resume control distinct from hard Stop",
    "description": "Idea surfaced while running the apra-fleet-7pm auto-sprint live: the
dashboard currently only exposes a hard 'Stop' (POST /stop, forcibly cancels
the workflow via `FleetWorkflow.requestStop()` -> aborts every in-flight
`agent()`/`command()` via `AbortController`, see
packages/apra-fleet-workflow/src/viewer/index.mjs:521 and
src/workflow/index.mjs:460). We verified the system is ALREADY resumable in
practice at the granularity of a full process restart -- beads (task state)
and git (commits) are the durable source of truth, so relaunching the
identical CLI invocation after a stop/crash correctly skips already-closed
tasks and picks up remaining open work.

What's missing is a deliberate, first-class pause/resume mechanism that is
cheaper and more graceful than "kill the process and relaunch," and that
resumes the SAME run rather than re-deriving everything from beads state and
re-running Plan from scratch.

## Key finding: the substrate for this already exists

`packages/apra-fleet-workflow`'s engine already has a journal/replay system
(`journal.mjs`, `computeActivityKey`, `journalEnabled`, `completedByKey`,
`diverged`) that backs instant-replay resume for `agent()`/`command()` calls
-- this is the same mechanism the Workflow tool's `resumeFromRunId` exposes:
re-running a script replays every already-completed activity from the
journal cache instantly, and only the first new/changed call executes live.
Pause/resume does not need a new persistence layer -- it needs a new
SUSPEND primitive built on the journal, distinct from both normal
completion and `requestStop()`'s hard cancellation.

## Proposed design: `interrupt()` as a journaled activity type

Modeled on LangGraph's `interrupt()` (raises inside a running graph node,
checkpoints state, halts until a human supplies a resume value, then
continues that exact node with the value in hand):

1. **New script-level primitive**: `await interrupt(checkpointName, {
   message, schema? })`, callable from `runner.js` at safe boundaries only
   (between cycles, after Deploy before Integ-Test, before Publish PR --
   never mid-agent-dispatch). Safe boundaries matter: unlike a hard Stop,
   Pause must never leave an `agent()` call half-abandoned, so it can only
   suspend at points the script itself designates, not at arbitrary
   moments an external Pause button is pressed.

2. **On first execution** (not yet in the journal): `interrupt()` writes a
   new journal entry of type `interrupt` (sequence-numbered like `agent`/
   `command` entries), containing `checkpointName`, `message`, and current
   script-level state useful for the dashboard to render (phase, cycle,
   round, streak -- the same state bead 9ub already names). It then emits
   an `interrupt` event (mirrors the existing `state`/`log`/`end` events in
   viewer/index.mjs) and the run's `executeFile()` promise resolves with
   status `'paused'` -- a new terminal status distinct from `'completed'`,
   `'cancelled'`, `'failed'`. Critically this is NOT `requestStop()`: no
   `AbortController` fires, nothing in flight is rejected, because by
   construction nothing is in flight at a designated checkpoint.

3. **Dashboard**: a `Pause` control becomes meaningful as "pause at the
   next checkpoint" (sets a flag the next `interrupt()` call checks) rather
   than "pause now" -- this is the honest UX given #1's constraint, and
   should be presented as such (e.g. "Pausing after current phase..." while
   waiting, not an instant pause). Once paused, the dashboard renders the
   `interrupt` event's `message`/checkpoint like a card, with a `Resume`
   button (and, if `schema` was supplied, an input form -- mirrors
   LangGraph's `Command(resume=value)` payload; e.g. a checkpoint before
   Publish PR could ask "proceed with PR title: <draft>?" and accept an
   edited title as the resume value).

4. **Resume**: `POST /resume { runId, value }` re-invokes
   `WorkflowEngine.executeFile()` with `journalEnabled: true` and the
   persisted journal for `runId`. Every activity before the `interrupt()`
   call (all completed `agent()`/`command()` calls, and the `interrupt`
   entry itself) replays from cache -- cheap, no re-dispatch, no re-running
   Plan. When the script reaches the SAME `interrupt()` call again, instead
   of suspending a second time, it returns the supplied `value` (or
   `undefined` if none was needed) and the script continues live from
   there. This is the exact mechanism that already makes `resumeFromRunId`
   work for `agent()` -- `interrupt()` is "the same replay machinery,
   generalized to a script-level suspend point instead of just an agent
   dispatch."

5. **`bin/cli.mjs` surface**: a `--resume-run-id <id>` flag, so the CLI
   launch this feeds from the dashboard doesn't need to reconstruct any
   auto-sprint-specific state -- it just hands the runId to
   `executeFile()` and the journal does the rest.

## Open questions / scoping notes (why this stays a backlog idea, not
scheduled work)

- Where exactly in `runner.js` do `interrupt()` checkpoints make sense?
  Candidates: end of each outer cycle, after Deploy succeeds and before
  Integ-Test runs (a natural "confirm before touching anything
  test-adjacent" point), before Publish PR (confirm PR title/description).
  Needs a pass through the phase list to pick boundaries that are both
  safe (no abandoned in-flight work) and useful (a human would actually
  want to intervene there).
- Interaction with the existing phase-resume/concurrency-lock work already
  landed in apra-pm (PR #19) -- needs a check that a second `executeFile()`
  invocation for the same `runId` doesn't race the lock that work put in
  place.
- Journal storage lifetime/location for a PAUSED (not finished) run --
  existing journal files are presumably write-once-per-run; pausing needs
  the journal kept live/appendable across the gap between suspend and
  resume, including across a full process restart if the dashboard's Node
  process itself needs to come down while paused.
- Whether `interrupt()` should be a generic `packages/apra-fleet-workflow`
  engine primitive (usable by any workflow script) or something auto-sprint
  specific in `runner.js` -- leaning toward the former since the journal
  machinery it rides on lives in the engine, not in auto-sprint.

Do not start without further design discussion -- this description is a
starting proposal, not an approved spec.
",

    "id": "apra-fleet-1cb",
    "title": "[bug] test mocks conflate 'shell exit nonzero' with MCP-level isError, unlike real execute_command",
    "description": "Discovered while fixing apra-fleet-7ll (execute_command structuredContent). Multiple apra-fleet-se test mock fleet APIs (advanced-mock-runner-test.mjs, budget-live.test.mjs, golden-transcript.test.mjs, golden-transcript-3bead.test.mjs) implement their bd/git command mocking as:

  const { err, stdout, stderr } = await runCmd(opts.command, tempDir);
  if (err) return { isError: true, content: [{ text: stderr || err.message }] };
  return { content: [{ text: stdout }] };

runCmd() wraps Node's child_process.exec(), whose callback receives a non-null `err` for ANY nonzero exit code, not just spawn-level failures. So these mocks treat "bd exited nonzero" as an MCP-level isError:true failure.

Real src/tools/execute-command.ts does NOT do this: its synchronous success path (result.code !== 0 branch) still returns a normal (non-isError) response with `Exit code: N\n<output>` text and (after apra-fleet-7ll) structuredContent -- a nonzero shell exit is just data on a successful dispatch, not a dispatch-level error. isError is reserved for actual dispatch failures (credential/network blocks, launch exceptions).

This means these mocks are more lenient in one place (never distinguish spawn failure from nonzero exit) and stricter in another (treat nonzero exit as a hard MCP error) than reality -- any runner.js code path that relies on receiving a normal (non-error) response for a nonzero-exit bd command would pass against these mocks today but could behave differently against the real server. Not yet confirmed to cause an actual runner.js bug; filed for investigation, not proven broken.

Deferred (P3): the immediate apra-fleet-7ll fix does not depend on resolving this; low priority since no concrete runner.js bug has been traced to it yet.",
    "acceptance_criteria": "Investigate whether any runner.js code path depends on receiving isError:true specifically for a nonzero-exit bd/git command (vs. treating it as normal data).   {
    "id": "apra-fleet-dmy",
    "title": "[follow-up] Drift detection: echo resolved model/tier on execute_prompt, verify against get_member_model_pricing (P4, not a prerequisite for dv5.5/.6)",
    "description": "REVISED 2026-07-12: SUPERSEDED as the primary cost-accuracy mechanism. Per project-owner correction 3 on epic apra-fleet-dv5, real per-member cost tracking is now solved WITHOUT needing per-dispatch echo-back: tier->model resolution is deterministic and config-driven (src/tools/execute-prompt.ts resolveModelForTier(), each provider's modelTiers()/modelForTier()), so a new `get_member_model_pricing` MCP tool (apra-fleet-dv5.5) can answer "what does member X's premium tier cost" ahead of time, once per member per run, with no echo-back required. apra-fleet-dv5.6 wires that into apra-fleet-workflow's budget tracking as the primary pricing source, falling back to pricing.mjs's tier-band estimate only when unavailable.

This issue is NOT closed as invalid -- it retains narrower residual value: DRIFT DETECTION. A member's actual runtime resolution could theoretically diverge from its registered model_tiers config (e.g. a provider-side override, a manually-edited settings file the fleet server doesn't know about, a mid-run config change) in ways get_member_model_pricing's static/cached lookup would not catch. Echo-back would let the client verify "the model I was actually billed against matches the model I priced against" rather than trusting the static resolution stays true for the whole run. This is a correctness/verification enhancement on TOP of dv5.5/dv5.6's primary mechanism, not a prerequisite for it.

Scope (files, if/when taken up): src/tools/execute-prompt.ts (already computes resolvedModel/resolvedTier ~293-329 -- include both in the tool's result payload), packages/apra-fleet-client (surface them on the execute_prompt response), packages/apra-fleet-workflow/src/workflow/index.mjs (compare echoed resolvedModel against the pre-dispatch get_member_model_pricing-based price; log/flag a mismatch rather than silently trusting either source).

Priority lowered to P4: no longer blocking real per-member costing (that's apra-fleet-dv5.5/.6); this is now a drift-detection nice-to-have. Depends conceptually on apra-fleet-dv5.5/.6 landing first (there is no static price to compare an echo against otherwise).

Risks/unknowns: journal/replay compatibility for new activity fields; remote/relayed members (hub-spoke RelayStrategy) may not have the resolved model available at the same point; mixed fleets where some members echo and some do not must not double-count or fabricate.

Acceptance criteria (for the eventual implementer): a dispatch against a member with model_tiers configured shows the concrete resolved model in the workflow journal; if it disagrees with the price pre-computed via get_member_model_pricing, a warning is logged naming the discrepancy; absent an echo, behavior is unaffected (dv5.5/.6's static resolution is used as-is, unchanged).

📋 Response from fleet-reorg:

Planning pass complete. The DAG was already structurally sound, so this was a targeted delta addressing the four review findings — I created no new beads and added no scope.

## Findings addressed

**(2) CRITERION 10 — model key inconsistency: FIXED.** Set the `model` key via `--set-metadata` (merge, not replace, so existing metadata survived) on the five parents that lacked it, using the three literal tier strings:

| bead | tier | rationale |
|---|---|---|
| `p4f` | premium | multi-file classification threading |
| `02s` | premium | cross-cutting, three distinct findings |
| `j6i` | standard | focused retry wrapper |
| `ire` | standard | new installer module |
| `0pu` | cheap | log-line change |

All 25 open beads in scope now carry a valid tier; audit confirms `MISSING: none`, `INVALID: none`. Consistent with `xbu.2`, which already had it.

**(3) SCOPE — `ire`: DOCUMENTED, decision escalated.** I did **not** unilaterally defer or bless it. `apra-fleet-ire` already existed and was already parented under `xbu` before this pass, so the manifest text was stale documentation of an existing fact — recording it is not new scope, whereas deferring it *would* have been a scope decision that isn't mine to make. I added an "Additions to scope" section to the manifest stating plainly that ire was filed after the goal list was written, that it is **not** a ruggedization bug but net-new installer capability, and that it is pending an explicit keep-or-defer call by the sprint owner before Develop.

**(1) DISPATCH HAZARD — cannot fix from planner; needs orchestrator action.** `apra-fleet-j6i.1` and `apra-fleet-6fr.1` are open, ready, and childless, so the dispatch-time parent filter won't exclude them — each will burn a doer dispatch on a confirmed no-op. I'm barred from closing beads, so I verified the underlying claims instead of leaving them on trust:
- `3346e3f` exists — `fix(auto-sprint): add timeout_s:900 to 6 dispatch types missing it (apra-fleet-j6i)` ✓
- `474ed88` exists — `fix(ci): share isolated HOME between workflow hello-world and arg/error-surface smoke steps` ✓ (message doesn't name 6fr, but matches the shared-FAKEHOME fix described)

**Both claims check out.** Regression coverage lives in `j6i.3` and `6fr.2`, so closing loses nothing. **Action required: close both as already-fixed before Develop, or exclude them at dispatch.**

**(4) ADVISORY — closed-parent/open-child: left as-is.** Affects `6fr`, `xbu.1`, `xbu.3`, `xbu.4`. Legal, non-deadlocking, and reopening parents to tidy tracking would be a status change the reviewer explicitly called non-blocking. Noted, not touched.

## Validation

```
bd list --parent apra-fleet-xbu --ready  -> 19 ready (non-empty, no cycle)
bd dep cycles                            -> no cycles detected
blocks crossing parent-child             -> none
```

All 5 blocked beads trace to a ready root (`xbu.4.1`, `xbu.2.1`, `xbu.3.1`). Every decomposed item retains ≥1 `[impl]` and exactly one `[test]` task gated behind its impl siblings.

**One thing needs your decision before Develop:** whether `apra-fleet-ire` (4 tasks, P2, dolt installer) belongs in this sprint at all. It's currently in scope and will be worked.

Independently verified against current packages/apra-fleet-se/auto-sprint/runner.js.\n\nOriginal claim (2026-07-15): Pre-sprint validation hard-fails on 'zero ready beads in scope' with no distinction between 'sprint genuinely complete' and 'stale cyclic DAG left by an interrupted prior planning pass' -- every relaunch just hard-fails at the same gate, with no self-heal path. Suggested fix: teach pre-sprint validation to detect a cycle specifically (vs. genuinely-zero work), and either auto-repair the common 2-node parent+blocks case, or route into a Planner delta-repair pass instead of throwing.\n\nCurrent state (verified 2026-07-16): the DETECTION half is now implemented (runner.js:1530-1550) -- it specifically scans not-done beads for a 'blocks' dependency that is also a parent-child ancestor/descendant pair, and if found, throws a distinctly-worded error naming the exact offending bead pairs AND the exact 'bd dep remove \u003ca\u003e \u003cb\u003e' fix command(s) needed, instead of the old generic 'no ready beads' message.     "title": "[bug] execute_prompt timeouts bypass retry entirely; runner.js conflates dispatch vs schema errors; schema-repair must resume session instead of re-sending full context (wastes tokens)",
    "description": "TRIAGED 2026-07-16: all three findings verified still present in current source; only line numbers have drifted (unrelated commits added lines above these regions since filing).\n\nFollow-up investigation (originally 2026-07-15) after diagnosing apra-fleet-j6i's Final Review timeout/FAIL. THREE distinct, confirmed findings: two are bugs (server-side retry-bypass, client-side error-type conflation), one is a decided fix (schema-repair should use session resume, not fresh self-contained re-asks -- confirmed by user 2026-07-15: \"must use resume, else we are wasting tokens\").\n\n1) SERVER-SIDE BUG (execute-prompt.ts) -- CONFIRMED, line unchanged: the outer catch block (now verified at execute-prompt.ts:506-508) still sets structuredContent:{isError:true, reason:'dispatch_failed'} for a genuine command timeout/thrown exception, structurally separate from and unreachable from the two retry mechanisms in the preceding try block (stale-session retry, server/overloaded-error retry) -- both of which only fire on a non-throwing nonzero exit, never on a thrown exception. A genuine timeout throws, so it reaches neither retry path -- single-shot, unconditionally-unretried, for every dispatch site system-wide.\n\n2) CLIENT-SIDE BUG (runner.js) -- CONFIRMED, now 6 combined-catch sites (was cited as \"at minimum Final Review's, ~line 2201\"; verified 2026-07-16 the same `err instanceof AgentOutputError || err instanceof AgentDispatchError` combined-branch pattern exists at runner.js:1249, 1740, 2161, 2218, 2398 (Final Review, was ~2201), and 2462). packages/apra-fleet-workflow/src/workflow/index.mjs still correctly types AgentDispatchError vs AgentOutputError distinctly (confirmed: index.mjs's doc comments and throw sites unchanged in intent), but runner.js's catch blocks still discard that distinction, reporting both as \"schema-repair exhausted, treating as FAIL\" regardless of which actually occurred.\n\n3) DECIDED FIX -- schema-repair must use session resume, not a fresh self-contained re-ask (packages/apra-fleet-workflow/src/workflow/index.mjs) -- CONFIRMED, lines verified: repair loop at index.mjs:720 (`for (let attempt = 0; attempt \u003c= maxRepairs; attempt++)`), `isRepair` computed at :728, still dispatches with a STATIC `resume: opts.resume ?? false` at :761 regardless of isRepair. The \"F10\" self-contained-re-ask rationale comment is still present (now ~:759-761 region). maxRepairs itself computed at :625 (`opts.schemaRetries ?? 2`).\n\nSUGGESTED FIX (full scope, unchanged from original investigation): (1) execute-prompt.ts's outer catch (dispatch exception path) should have its own bounded retry (at minimum one retry with a fresh session, mirroring the existing stale-session-retry pattern) rather than being an unconditional single-shot failure. (2) runner.js's 6 combined-catch sites should branch on the error TYPE (AgentDispatchError vs AgentOutputError) and handle each distinctly -- an AgentDispatchError should trigger a dispatch-retry or a clearly-labeled \"infrastructure failure, not a quality failure\" outcome, never be conflated with schema-repair exhaustion. (3) switch index.mjs's schema-repair loop to force resume:true when isRepair===true and shrink buildRepairPrompt() to a lean reminder-only message (validation errors + \"please respond again with corrected JSON only\" -- not re-embedding the full original prompt/invalid output) -- verify session continuity holds specifically across repair rounds before shipping.",

    "description": "src/cli/install.ts (~line 1040, the 'Beads install step') currently installs only @beads/bd via npm global install, with no dolt binary install. PoC (2026-07-16, dolt-poc-v2) confirmed that having a real dolt binary available (portable download, no admin/system install needed -- dolt v2.2.0 windows-amd64.zip, ~40MB download, single ~123MB static exe, no dependencies) and spinning up a temporary 'dolt sql-server' pointed directly at bd's existing '.beads/embeddeddolt' data dir (no migration needed) unlocks the documented dolt_conflicts / dolt_conflicts_resolve resolve-in-place path for Dolt merge conflicts -- which is otherwise completely blocked in bd's embedded mode ('bd sql'/'bd doctor' both hard-refuse). Measured footprint: ~73MB idle RSS, ~1.6s startup, single configurable port (default 3306), no system-wide footprint -- cheap enough to install unconditionally and spin up on-demand only when a conflict is actually detected (no persistent daemon required). Add a fleet-installer step (mirroring the existing Beads install step's already-installed-check pattern) that downloads/extracts the dolt binary to a per-install location (e.g. alongside the existing BIN_DIR, not system PATH) and verifies it via 'dolt version', then smoke-tests that a real 'dolt sql-server' can start against a scratch data dir and accept a connection. See the auto-sprint-service design plan (https://claude.ai/code/artifact/413dd5a5-68f1-4a46-b10f-992dff241d3f, Part 3.4) for the full verified PoC findings and the exact conflict-resolution command ritual this unlocks.",

    "description": "TRIAGED 2026-07-16: all three findings verified still present in current source; only line numbers have drifted (unrelated commits added lines above these regions since filing).\n\nFollow-up investigation (originally 2026-07-15) after diagnosing apra-fleet-j6i's Final Review timeout/FAIL. THREE distinct, confirmed findings: two are bugs (server-side retry-bypass, client-side error-type conflation), one is a decided fix (schema-repair should use session resume, not fresh self-contained re-asks -- confirmed by user 2026-07-15: \"must use resume, else we are wasting tokens\").\n\n1) SERVER-SIDE BUG (execute-prompt.ts) -- CONFIRMED, line unchanged: the outer catch block (now verified at execute-prompt.ts:506-508) still sets structuredContent:{isError:true, reason:'dispatch_failed'} for a genuine command timeout/thrown exception, structurally separate from and unreachable from the two retry mechanisms in the preceding try block (stale-session retry, server/overloaded-error retry) -- both of which only fire on a non-throwing nonzero exit, never on a thrown exception. A genuine timeout throws, so it reaches neither retry path -- single-shot, unconditionally-unretried, for every dispatch site system-wide.\n\n2) CLIENT-SIDE BUG (runner.js) -- CONFIRMED, now 6 combined-catch sites (was cited as \"at minimum Final Review's, ~line 2201\"; verified 2026-07-16 the same `err instanceof AgentOutputError || err instanceof AgentDispatchError` combined-branch pattern exists at runner.js:1249, 1740, 2161, 2218, 2398 (Final Review, was ~2201), and 2462). packages/apra-fleet-workflow/src/workflow/index.mjs still correctly types AgentDispatchError vs AgentOutputError distinctly (confirmed: index.mjs's doc comments and throw sites unchanged in intent), but runner.js's catch blocks still discard that distinction, reporting both as \"schema-repair exhausted, treating as FAIL\" regardless of which actually occurred.\n\n3) DECIDED FIX -- schema-repair must use session resume, not a fresh self-contained re-ask (packages/apra-fleet-workflow/src/workflow/index.mjs) -- CONFIRMED, lines verified: repair loop at index.mjs:720 (`for (let attempt = 0; attempt \u003c= maxRepairs; attempt++)`), `isRepair` computed at :728, still dispatches with a STATIC `resume: opts.resume ?? false` at :761 regardless of isRepair. The \"F10\" self-contained-re-ask rationale comment is still present (now ~:759-761 region). maxRepairs itself computed at :625 (`opts.schemaRetries ?? 2`).\n\nSUGGESTED FIX (full scope, unchanged from original investigation): (1) execute-prompt.ts's outer catch (dispatch exception path) should have its own bounded retry (at minimum one retry with a fresh session, mirroring the existing stale-session-retry pattern) rather than being an unconditional single-shot failure.     "description": "src/cli/install.ts (~line 1040, the 'Beads install step') currently installs only @beads/bd via npm global install, with no dolt binary install. ParsedResponse (src/providers/provider.ts:42-48) is still exactly {result, sessionId?, isError, raw, usage?} -- no subtype/terminalReason/errors field. src/providers/claude.ts:76-127 parseResponse() still parses the full CLI JSON (JSON array / single object / JSONL formats) and discards obj.subtype/obj.terminal_reason/obj.errors at every return site. src/providers/claude.ts:42 still `const turns = maxTurns ?? 50`.\n2. src/utils/prompt-errors.ts:1-15 classifyPromptError() still has exactly 'auth'/'server'/'overloaded' + catch-all 'unknown' -- no 'max_turns' category. isRetryable() still true only for 'server'/'overloaded'. Exit code: 0
[
  {
    "id": "apra-fleet-qv1",
    "title": "[bug][important] Parallel doer streak dispatch to a single member is unsafe -- causes hours-long hangs + response corruption; needs worktree-per-streak redesign",
    "description": "Auto-sprint's Develop phase can assign multiple doer streaks to run "in
parallel" within one round (e.g. `Develop C1 R1` on 2026-07-15 assigned
streaks `[["apra-fleet-fih.2"],["apra-fleet-fih.3"]]`). Today this caused a
live sprint (`apra-fleet-fih`) to stall for over an hour with zero forward
progress: both streaks were dispatched concurrently to the SAME fleet
member (`fleet-reorg`), which is a single working-directory/checkout, not
an isolated session per dispatch.

## Constraint (confirmed by user, authoritative)

A member = one folder/checkout. It is NOT possible to safely run two
concurrent `execute_prompt` dispatches against the same member -- there is
no isolation between them (shared git checkout, shared session/process).

The only correct way to achieve genuine parallel doer work is: register
multiple members, each pointed at its own git worktree, dispatch one
streak per member/worktree, and after all streaks complete, merge the
worktree branches back with proper branch hygiene (clean up worktrees,
resolve/avoid conflicts, delete merged branches). This is real
infrastructure work (worktree lifecycle + member registration automation +
post-merge cleanup), not a one-line fix.

## What happened without this (observed failure mode)

Two concurrent dispatches against one shared-folder member produced:
- Repeated 900s inactivity timeouts on both streaks (contention for the
  same folder/session -- likely git index lock or transport-level
  single-invocation assumption).
- One dispatch's response came back with a corrupted multi-byte UTF-8
  sequence (`📋` emoji prefix decoded as U+FFFD replacement character),
  breaking JSON schema-repair parsing. Plausibly caused by output
  interleaving between the two concurrent invocations against a channel
  the transport assumes is single-occupancy per member.
- One streak (`fih.2`) left a real, syntactically-valid but unverified
  partial edit in the working tree when its dispatch died mid-response --
  recovered and committed as a WIP checkpoint
  (`34f2752` on `test/apra-fleet-se-speedup`) rather than lost.

## Immediate stopgap (do before the real fix, cheap and safe)

Until worktree-per-streak isolation exists, `runner.js`'s Develop-phase
streak dispatch must not fan out more than one streak concurrently against
a single member. Either serialize streak dispatch (one at a time, same
member) or hard-fail pre-sprint validation if the streak-assignment output
would produce >1 streak with only one member available/registered for the
scope. Silent concurrent dispatch against one folder must not be possible
-- it should fail loud, not hang for an hour.

## Real fix (needs its own planning pass, do not attempt inline)

1. Member-per-worktree provisioning: given a scope with N ready parallel
   streaks, auto-create N git worktrees (or reuse pre-registered
   worktree-backed members) before Develop phase dispatch.
2. Route each streak's doer dispatch to its own worktree-backed member.
3. Post-round merge/branch-hygiene step: after all streaks in a round
   complete (success or failure), merge each worktree's branch back into
   the sprint branch, handle conflicts (fail loud + surface to reviewer,
   do not auto-resolve), then remove the now-merged worktrees so they don't
   accumulate across rounds/sprints.
4. Decide member registration lifecycle: are worktree-backed members
   ephemeral (registered/deregistered per sprint) or a fixed pool reused
   across sprints? Affects `~/.apra-fleet/data/registry.json` growth and
   whether stale worktree directories need periodic GC.
5. Update `doer.md`/`planner.md` prose contracts if the scope/member
   assignment model changes as part of this (may intersect with the
   graph-consistency remediation plan's Phase B/C work already tracked
   separately).

Acceptance criteria for this backlog item once planned: a sprint that
assigns 2+ parallel doer streaks completes them in genuinely isolated
worktrees, merges cleanly with no manual intervention required in the
common case, and any merge conflict is surfaced as a loud, actionable
failure rather than silent data loss or an hour-long hang.
",

  {
    "id": "apra-fleet-5fi",
    "title": "[feature] Inject role-prefixed bd memories (doer-memory-*, reviewer-memory-*, ...) into auto-sprint dispatches -- operator-extensible prompting without a submodule PR",
    "description": "## Problem

There is currently no way for a repo operator to superimpose extra prose
guidance onto a dispatched role (doer, reviewer, streak-assignment,
integ-test-runner, etc.) without editing that role's prompt contract file
directly (`vendor/apra-pm/agents/<role>.md`) -- which requires committing
inside the `vendor/apra-pm` git submodule, opening a PR there, merging it,
then bumping the submodule pointer in `apra-fleet-reorg` and rebuilding
`dist/agents/*.md` before the change takes effect. This is the correct,
heavyweight path for stable, designed contract changes -- but it is far too
slow for a quick, project-specific operator note (e.g. "on this machine,
always double-quote `bd list --status=` filters" or "never busy-wait poll a
backgrounded test command for more than 60s without emitting a status
line") that should take effect on the very next dispatch.

This session hit multiple real examples of exactly this kind of gotcha
(the PowerShell comma-array-mangling bug, the member-is-one-folder
parallel-dispatch constraint, the busy-wait/inactivity-timeout interaction)
-- each would have been directly prevented if a prior sprint's learning had
been immediately available to the next dispatch, with no rebuild cycle.

## Proposed mechanism

Reuse `bd`'s existing memory feature (`bd remember` / `bd memories`,
already used for cross-session operator knowledge -- see `bd prime`'s
"Persistent Memories" block) as the storage and retrieval layer, with a
role-prefixed key convention:

- `bd remember --key doer-memory-<slug> "..."`
- `bd remember --key reviewer-memory-<slug> "..."`
- `bd remember --key planner-memory-<slug> "..."`
- (one prefix per dispatched role/agentType that `runner.js` supports --
  enumerate all: planner, plan-reviewer, doer, reviewer, integ-test-runner,
  deployer, harvester)

Scope: **global**, stored in the project's own beads DB (not
sprint-specific, not bead/item-specific -- confirmed explicitly out of
scope by the repo owner). A memory written today is visible to every
future sprint run against this repo, indefinitely, until updated/removed.

## Integration point

In `packages/apra-fleet-se/auto-sprint/runner.js`, at each dispatch call
site (the same ones identified for the `max_total_s` ceiling work: Doer,
Reviewer x2, Integ-Test-Runner, plus Planner, Plan-Reviewer, Deployer,
Harvester for completeness), before building the dispatch prompt:

1. Run `bd memories <role>-memory --json` (e.g. `bd memories doer-memory
   --json`) to fetch any matching operator memories for that role.
2. If any are returned, append them to the prompt under a clearly-labeled,
   distinct section (e.g. `## Project-specific operator notes`) so the
   model understands this is supplementary, operator-authored guidance --
   not part of the base agent contract, and not to be confused with
   untrusted agent-output content (no `wrapUntrustedBlock` fencing needed;
   this is operator-authored, trusted content, same trust level as the
   rest of the dispatch prompt).
3. If no memories match, the section is omitted entirely -- no empty
   headers, no behavior change from today for a project that hasn't
   adopted this convention yet.

## Documentation requirement (explicit part of this bead's scope)

This is as important as the code: document the existence of this
extensibility point clearly, so operators actually discover and use it
instead of reaching for the heavyweight submodule-PR path out of habit.
At minimum:

- Add a section to `packages/apra-fleet-se/auto-sprint/docs/README.md`
  (the existing "how to run auto-sprint correctly" doc) explaining the
  `<role>-memory-<slug>` convention, with a concrete example
  (`bd remember --key doer-memory-powershell-quoting "..."`), and stating
  explicitly that this is global/repo-wide, takes effect on the next
  dispatch with no rebuild, and is the preferred path for
  operational/empirical gotchas vs. the submodule-PR path for stable
  contract design changes.
- Note the enumerated list of valid role prefixes (must match `runner.js`'s
  actual `agentType` values exactly, or a typo'd key silently never
  matches anything -- consider whether `runner.js` should also emit a
  one-line log/warning listing which memory keys (if any) were actually
  applied to a given dispatch, purely for operator visibility/debugging,
  not enforcement).

## Acceptance criteria

- A `bd remember --key doer-memory-<slug> "..."` memory is visibly
  included in the next Doer dispatch's prompt (verifiable via the sprint
  log's own dispatch-prompt narration, or a direct unit test against the
  prompt-building function).
- No matching memories -> no section appended, no error, no change from
  current behavior.
- All roles enumerated above are covered, not just doer/reviewer.
- `packages/apra-fleet-se/auto-sprint/docs/README.md` documents the
  convention with a working example.
- Existing `advanced-mock-runner-test.mjs`/split successor tests still
  pass unmodified (this is additive prompt content, not a change to
  dispatch control flow) -- add new test coverage for the injection
  itself rather than relying on existing tests to incidentally catch it.

## Explicitly out of scope for this bead

- Sprint-specific or bead/item-specific memory scoping (e.g.
  `doer-memory-<bead-id>-<slug>`) -- confirmed not needed right now.
- Any UI/dashboard surface for authoring memories -- `bd remember` on the
  CLI is sufficient.
- Automatic memory *authoring* by the harvester or reviewer roles (a
  separate, related idea discussed the same session -- track as its own
  follow-up bead if pursued, do not conflate with this one, which is only
  about the *retrieval/injection* mechanism for memories a human already
  wrote).
",

- dist/agents/planner.md: Step 4 'Acyclicity check (mandatory)' (~line 154) -- add the same caveat and reaffirm the scoped 'bd list --parent <scope> --ready --json' non-empty check is the real signal, not an assumed-unnecessary check for non-epic scopes.
