backlogTasks.title: Template a pre-initialized empty bd directory for test setup() calls
backlogTasks.description: Every heavy test scenario's setup() (advanced-mock-runner-test.mjs, budget-live.test.mjs, golden-transcript*.test.mjs) runs 'bd init' plus several 'bd create'/'bd update --parent' round-trips from scratch per scenario -- real subprocess-spawn and Dolt-DB bootstrap cost repeated 25+ times. Investigated reusing the pre-seeded fleet-e2e-toy repo's beads DB for this (2026-07-15): NOT viable -- its 7-8 issues are a flat, unrelated toy CLI backlog with no parent-child hierarchy, dependency edges, or --metadata model-tier fields, none of which match what these unit-mock scenarios specifically need to exercise (reject/reopen paths, 3-bead sort ordering, etc.); copying its content would save nothing. The real, smaller optimization: template a pre-initialized, empty-schema .beads/ directory (just past 'bd init', no seeded issues) and copy it fresh per scenario instead of re-running bd init's own bootstrap from scratch every time -- independent of fleet-e2e-toy, which doesn't help here.

backlogTasks.title: [test] round-cap exit logs a distinct 'deferring to next cycle' message, not 'Looping back'
backlogTasks.description:
Add automated coverage for the auto-sprint Develop/Review loop-exit logging (apra-fleet-0pu) proving the two exit paths are distinguishable from the log alone.

What to test:
- Round-cap path: with devRounds reaching 3 while stillOpen/currentReady remains non-empty, the runner emits an explicit 'round cap reached ... deferring to next cycle' log line (including the still-open bead count) and does NOT emit the misleading 'Looping back to develop' as the terminating line.
- Organic-completion path: when stillOpen.length === 0, the runner emits a distinct 'cycle organically complete' style message and exits without the round-cap line.

How to assert pass/fail: drive the loop logic in packages/apra-fleet-se/auto-sprint/runner.js with the existing runner test harness (mock bd list --ready results to control stillOpen), capturing emitted log lines and asserting on their text. If runner.js has no unit-test seam, factor the minimal check or assert via the harness already used by other runner tests. Framework: vitest (npm test).

Acceptance criteria:
- Test fails against pre-fix behavior (no distinct round-cap line) and passes after apra-fleet-0pu impl lands.
- Both exit paths asserted: round-cap-with-open-work and organic-completion.
- Confirms carry-forward of reopened beads is unchanged (no bead lost at the cap).
- Green under 'npm test'.

backlogTasks.title: [impl] Log an explicit 'Develop/Review round cap reached' message at loop exit
backlogTasks.description:
In the auto-sprint runner (packages/apra-fleet-se/auto-sprint/runner.js, the per-cycle 'while (devRounds < 3)' Develop/Review loop, ~lines 1689 and 1959-1964), add an explicit log line that fires when the loop exits because the round cap was hit with work still open -- distinct from the organic 'stillOpen.length === 0' completion.

Current bug (apra-fleet-0pu): when devRounds reaches 3 with beads still open/reopened, the loop logs the misleading 'System found N beads still open/ready. Looping back to develop.' and then silently exits into Deploy on the next while-condition check -- the 'Looping back' message implies the opposite of what actually happens.

Files to change: packages/apra-fleet-se/auto-sprint/runner.js (single file).

Suggested fix: after the while loop exits, detect the case devRounds === 3 AND the last iteration's stillOpen/currentReady was non-empty, and emit an explicit line such as: 'Develop/Review round cap (3) reached this cycle with N bead(s) still open/reopened -- deferring to next cycle.' Keep the organic 'stillOpen.length === 0' break path logging a distinct 'cycle organically complete' message. Do not change control flow or the carry-forward behavior (reopened beads already correctly flow to the next Sprint Cycle) -- this is a logging/observability fix only.

Acceptance criteria:
- Round-cap exit (devRounds===3 with work still open) logs a distinct, accurate 'round cap reached ... deferring to next cycle' line naming the count of still-open beads.
- The organic completion path (stillOpen empty) logs a clearly different message and no longer prints the misleading 'Looping back to develop' when the loop is actually terminating.
- No change to which beads carry into the next cycle; only log output changes.
- Existing runner behavior/tests unaffected.

backlogTasks.title: [bug] Dashboard Backlog panel shows empty despite real project-wide open/deferred/blocked beads existing
backlogTasks.description:
Live-verified 2026-07-15 on the running apra-fleet-7pm dashboard Beads Tasks tab (port 18300): Backlog section renders 'No backlog items.' At the same moment, a direct 'bd list --status=open,deferred,blocked --json' against the same working directory returned 25 real beads (including several I filed minutes earlier in this same session: apra-fleet-p4f, h7x, 0pu, k7s, 4p5, plus many pre-existing ones like apra-fleet-9ub, adl, 1cb, nbp, 4yr, 3ns.6, aw8, 92d).

The sprint log has zero occurrences of 'failed to refresh backlog panel' or 'failed to refresh sprint-tree panel' (the try/catch log lines added in the Sprint/Backlog updateDashboard() rewrite), so this is not a caught/logged fetch error -- the backlog fetch appears to either return empty, or its result is not reaching publishState/the browser correctly, or the panel is stale and simply never re-rendered after the beads in question were created (updateDashboard() runs once per Develop/Review round, and many rounds have elapsed since bead creation, so simple staleness alone seems insufficient to explain a fully-empty result).

Needs direct investigation: (1) confirm the orchestrator member's bd command actually queries the same .beads/ database as the manual check (should be identical for a local same-checkout member, but verify); (2) add temporary logging or re-run the exact 'bd list --status=open,deferred,blocked --json' command via the same command()/orchestratorMember path used by updateDashboard() to see what it actually returns; (3) check whether BACKLOG_STATUSES filtering or the sprintIds exclusion in updateDashboard() (packages/apra-fleet-se/auto-sprint/runner.js) has a bug that zeroes out the result. This is a live, reproducible discrepancy between real beads-DB state and what the dashboard displays -- filed as an observed symptom pending code-level root-cause.

backlogTasks.title: [UI] Backlog panel should render dependency tree, not flat list
backlogTasks.description: Sprint/Backlog dashboard (packages/apra-fleet-se/auto-sprint/viewer-extensions.mjs renderFlatRow/renderBeadsHtml) currently renders the Backlog section as a flat sorted list of rows. If backlog items have dependency relationships among themselves (dependencies[].type === 'blocks'), those should be rendered as a tree (same nesting approach as the Sprint section's renderNode) rather than flattened, so users can see backlog structure/blocking relationships at a glance. Items with no dependencies among the backlog set can remain flat/top-level. User feedback from live dashboard review session (2026-07-13/14).

backlogTasks.title: [UI] Beads dashboard tree nodes should be collapsible/expandable
backlogTasks.description: Sprint/Backlog dashboard tree (packages/apra-fleet-se/auto-sprint/viewer-extensions.mjs renderBeadsHtml) currently renders every node fully expanded with no way to collapse. Tree nodes (and the two top-level Sprint/Backlog section headers themselves) should be collapsible/expandable like standard tree-view UI, so users can fold away completed or uninteresting subtrees. User feedback from live dashboard review session (2026-07-13/14).

backlogTasks.title: Auto-sprint: add an explicit pause/resume control distinct from hard Stop
backlogTasks.description:
Idea surfaced while running the apra-fleet-7pm auto-sprint live: the
dashboard currently only exposes a hard 'Stop' (POST /stop, forcibly cancels
the workflow via `FleetWorkflow.requestStop()` -> aborts every in-flight
`agent()`/`command()` via `AbortController`, see
packages/apra-fleet-workflow/src/viewer/index.mjs:521 and
src/workflow/index.mjs:460). We verified the system is ALREADY resumable in
practice at the granularity of a full process restart -- beads (task state)
and git (commits) are the durable source of truth, so relaunching the
identical CLI invocation after a stop/crash correctly skips already-closed
tasks and picks up remaining open work.

What's missing is a deliberate, first-class pause/resume mechanism that is
cheaper and more graceful than "kill the process and relaunch," and that
resumes the SAME run rather than re-deriving everything from beads state and
re-running Plan from scratch.

## Key finding: the substrate for this already exists

`packages/apra-fleet-workflow`'s engine already has a journal/replay system
(`journal.mjs`, `computeActivityKey`, `journalEnabled`, `completedByKey`,
`diverged`) that backs instant-replay resume for `agent()`/`command()` calls
-- this is the same mechanism the Workflow tool's `resumeFromRunId` exposes:
re-running a script replays every already-completed activity from the
journal cache instantly, and only the first new/changed call executes live.
Pause/resume does not need a new persistence layer -- it needs a new
SUSPEND primitive built on the journal, distinct from both normal
completion and `requestStop()`'s hard cancellation.

## Proposed design: `interrupt()` as a journaled activity type

Modeled on LangGraph's `interrupt()` (raises inside a running graph node,
checkpoints state, halts until a human supplies a resume value, then
continues that exact node with the value in hand):

1. **New script-level primitive**: `await interrupt(checkpointName, {
   message, schema? })`, callable from `runner.js` at safe boundaries only
   (between cycles, after Deploy before Integ-Test, before Publish PR --
   never mid-agent-dispatch). Safe boundaries matter: unlike a hard Stop,
   Pause must never leave an `agent()` call half-abandoned, so it can only
   suspend at points the script itself designates, not at arbitrary
   moments an external Pause button is pressed.

2. **On first execution** (not yet in the journal): `interrupt()` writes a
   new journal entry of type `interrupt` (sequence-numbered like `agent`/
   `command` entries), containing `checkpointName`, `message`, and current
   script-level state useful for the dashboard to render (phase, cycle,
   round, streak -- the same state bead 9ub already names). It then emits
   an `interrupt` event (mirrors the existing `state`/`log`/`end` events in
   viewer/index.mjs) and the run's `executeFile()` promise resolves with
   status `'paused'` -- a new terminal status distinct from `'completed'`,
   `'cancelled'`, `'failed'`. Critically this is NOT `requestStop()`: no
   `AbortController` fires, nothing in flight is rejected, because by
   construction nothing is in flight at a designated checkpoint.

3. **Dashboard**: a `Pause` control becomes meaningful as "pause at the
   next checkpoint" (sets a flag the next `interrupt()` call checks) rather
   than "pause now" -- this is the honest UX given #1's constraint, and
   should be presented as such (e.g. "Pausing after current phase..." while
   waiting, not an instant pause). Once paused, the dashboard renders the
   `interrupt` event's `message`/checkpoint like a card, with a `Resume`
   button (and, if `schema` was supplied, an input form -- mirrors
   LangGraph's `Command(resume=value)` payload; e.g. a checkpoint before
   Publish PR could ask "proceed with PR title: <draft>?" and accept an
   edited title as the resume value).

4. **Resume**: `POST /resume { runId, value }` re-invokes
   `WorkflowEngine.executeFile()` with `journalEnabled: true` and the
   persisted journal for `runId`. Every activity before the `interrupt()`
   call (all completed `agent()`/`command()` calls, and the `interrupt`
   entry itself) replays from cache -- cheap, no re-dispatch, no re-running
   Plan. When the script reaches the SAME `interrupt()` call again, instead
   of suspending a second time, it returns the supplied `value` (or
   `undefined` if none was needed) and the script continues live from
   there. This is the exact mechanism that already makes `resumeFromRunId`
   work for `agent()` -- `interrupt()` is "the same replay machinery,
   generalized to a script-level suspend point instead of just an agent
   dispatch."

5. **`bin/cli.mjs` surface**: a `--resume-run-id <id>` flag, so the CLI
   launch this feeds from the dashboard doesn't need to reconstruct any
   auto-sprint-specific state -- it just hands the runId to
   `executeFile()` and the journal does the rest.

## Open questions / scoping notes (why this stays a backlog idea, not
scheduled work)

- Where exactly in `runner.js` do `interrupt()` checkpoints make sense?
  Candidates: end of each outer cycle, after Deploy succeeds and before
  Integ-Test runs (a natural "confirm before touching anything
  test-adjacent" point), before Publish PR (confirm PR title/description).
  Needs a pass through the phase list to pick boundaries that are both
  safe (no abandoned in-flight work) and useful (a human would actually
  want to intervene there).
- Interaction with the existing phase-resume/concurrency-lock work already
  landed in apra-pm (PR #19) -- needs a check that a second `executeFile()`
  invocation for the same `runId` doesn't race the lock that work put in
  place.
- Journal storage lifetime/location for a PAUSED (not finished) run --
  existing journal files are presumably write-once-per-run; pausing needs
  the journal kept live/appendable across the gap between suspend and
  resume, including across a full process restart if the dashboard's Node
  process itself needs to come down while paused.
- Whether `interrupt()` should be a generic `packages/apra-fleet-workflow`
  engine primitive (usable by any workflow script) or something auto-sprint
  specific in `runner.js` -- leaning toward the former since the journal
  machinery it rides on lives in the engine, not in auto-sprint.

Do not start without further design discussion -- this description is a
starting proposal, not an approved spec.

backlogTasks.title: [bug] pm-skill planner.md/plan-reviewer.md use literal Claude model names in task metadata, inconsistent with apra-fleet-se's cheap/standard/premium tier vocabulary
backlogTasks.description:
Discovered live during apra-fleet-7pm's real auto-sprint run (2026-07-13). The standalone `planner` and `plan-reviewer` subagents (installed from vendor/apra-pm's agents/planner.md, agents/plan-reviewer.md -- generic pm-skill agents, not apra-fleet-se-specific) write/expect task metadata like `{"model": "opus"}` / `{"model": "sonnet"}` -- literal Claude model family names.

But packages/apra-fleet-se/auto-sprint/runner.js's real dispatch (FIXED_ROLE_TIER, apra-fleet-dv5) expects the three literal tier strings 'cheap'/'standard'/'premium' instead -- de-hardcoding literal model names was the entire point of apra-fleet-dv5.

When I used the generic `planner` agent to build apra-fleet-7pm's DAG (ahead of running auto-sprint against it) and had `plan-reviewer` review it, both used the opus/sonnet vocabulary, matching planner.md's own documented convention. auto-sprint's OWN internal Plan phase (running as part of the real sprint, not the standalone agent) caught the mismatch and silently rewrote all 14 tasks' metadata to the tier vocabulary before proceeding -- so this round self-healed, but it cost an extra rewrite pass and could easily NOT self-heal for a differently-worded task set (there's no guarantee auto-sprint's internal planner always catches every task, e.g. if it deems some already fine and doesn't compare against the full ready-list from a fresh angle).

Root cause: vendor/apra-pm's planner.md/plan-reviewer.md were written independently of, and predate, apra-fleet-dv5's tier-vocabulary contract, and were never updated to match it.
backlogTasks.acceptance_criteria: Decide and document the correct relationship: either (a) update vendor/apra-pm's planner.md/plan-reviewer.md to use the cheap/standard/premium tier vocabulary when the target is an apra-fleet-se auto-sprint run (possibly conditioned on some signal distinguishing 'pm skill standalone sprint' from 'apra-fleet-se auto-sprint target'), or (b) make auto-sprint's runner.js Plan phase's self-correction an explicit, tested, always-on step (not incidental) with a warning surfaced when a rewrite happens, so users know their upstream planning tool used the wrong vocabulary. Either way, the two docs should stop silently disagreeing.

backlogTasks.title: [bug] test mocks conflate 'shell exit nonzero' with MCP-level isError, unlike real execute_command
backlogTasks.acceptance_criteria: Investigate whether any runner.js code path depends on receiving isError:true specifically for a nonzero-exit bd/git command (vs. treating it as normal data). If none do, update the affected mocks to only set isError on a genuine runCmd() spawn failure (e.g. err.code === undefined / ENOENT), matching real execute-command.ts semantics, and confirm the existing test suites still pass.

backlogTasks.title: [bug] parallel() replay.diverged flag is shared by reference across branch forks (divergence-propagation timing, not key-determinism)
backlogTasks.description: Follow-up from apra-fleet-unw2.14's review: replay.diverged is a single flag shared by reference across all parallel() branch store forks (pre-existing, not introduced by unw2.14's key-determinism fix). If a genuine divergence occurs in one branch while siblings are still mid-flight, those siblings could be forced live for calls that would have cache-hit, depending on scheduling. Orthogonal to N6 (key-computation determinism) -- this is about divergence-propagation timing/isolation across branches. Does not affect unw2.14's acceptance criteria. Scope: packages/apra-fleet-workflow/src/workflow/index.mjs's parallel() store-fork/divergence-flag handling. Consider per-branch divergence isolation if desired.

backlogTasks.title: Auto-sprint viewer: Stop uses old-school browser confirm()/alert() dialogs, needs modern styling
backlogTasks.description: Observed live: clicking 'Stop' in the workflow dashboard triggers two native browser dialogs in sequence -- a confirm() ('Are you sure you want to forcibly stop the workflow?') and then an alert() ('Stop signal sent.') once /stop resolves. These are unstyled OS-native dialogs that clash with the dashboard's otherwise modern dark-glass UI. The confirmation step itself should stay (don't remove the safety check before a forceful stop) -- just replace the native confirm()/alert() with an in-page modal/toast matching the existing --bg/--bg-glass/--accent/--danger CSS variables and visual language already used elsewhere in the dashboard (see the .btn-stop styling). Cosmetic, low priority. Reported by user observation; not yet investigated -- record only.
backlogTasks.notes: Deferred by user decision (2026-07-13): purely cosmetic, not urgent. Leave open, pick up later.

backlogTasks.title: [follow-up] Drift detection: echo resolved model/tier on execute_prompt, verify against get_member_model_pricing (P4, not a prerequisite for dv5.5/.6)
backlogTasks.description:
REVISED 2026-07-12: SUPERSEDED as the primary cost-accuracy mechanism. Per project-owner correction 3 on epic apra-fleet-dv5, real per-member cost tracking is now solved WITHOUT needing per-dispatch echo-back: tier->model resolution is deterministic and config-driven (src/tools/execute-prompt.ts resolveModelForTier(), each provider's modelTiers()/modelForTier()), so a new `get_member_model_pricing` MCP tool (apra-fleet-dv5.5) can answer "what does member X's premium tier cost" ahead of time, once per member per run, with no echo-back required. apra-fleet-dv5.6 wires that into apra-fleet-workflow's budget tracking as the primary pricing source, falling back to pricing.mjs's tier-band estimate only when unavailable.

This issue is NOT closed as invalid -- it retains narrower residual value: DRIFT DETECTION. A member's actual runtime resolution could theoretically diverge from its registered model_tiers config (e.g. a provider-side override, a manually-edited settings file the fleet server doesn't know about, a mid-run config change) in ways get_member_model_pricing's static/cached lookup would not catch. Echo-back would let the client verify "the model I was actually billed against matches the model I priced against" rather than trusting the static resolution stays true for the whole run. This is a correctness/verification enhancement on TOP of dv5.5/dv5.6's primary mechanism, not a prerequisite for it.

Scope (files, if/when taken up): src/tools/execute-prompt.ts (already computes resolvedModel/resolvedTier ~293-329 -- include both in the tool's result payload), packages/apra-fleet-client (surface them on the execute_prompt response), packages/apra-fleet-workflow/src/workflow/index.mjs (compare echoed resolvedModel against the pre-dispatch get_member_model_pricing-based price; log/flag a mismatch rather than silently trusting either source).

Priority lowered to P4: no longer blocking real per-member costing (that's apra-fleet-dv5.5/.6); this is now a drift-detection nice-to-have. Depends conceptually on apra-fleet-dv5.5/.6 landing first (there is no static price to compare an echo against otherwise).

Risks/unknowns: journal/replay compatibility for new activity fields; remote/relayed members (hub-spoke RelayStrategy) may not have the resolved model available at the same point; mixed fleets where some members echo and some do not must not double-count or fabricate.

Acceptance criteria (for the eventual implementer): a dispatch against a member with model_tiers configured shows the concrete resolved model in the workflow journal; if it disagrees with the price pre-computed via get_member_model_pricing, a warning is logged naming the discrepancy; absent an echo, behavior is unaffected (dv5.5/.6's static resolution is used as-is, unchanged).

backlogTasks.title: [DEFERRED] Relay/NAT-traversal command execution -- revisit once fleet-dashboard's bidirectional WebSocket relay ships
backlogTasks.description:
Explicitly deferred, not abandoned: fleet-dashboard's docs/architecture.md documents its own bidirectional WebSocket relay (member <-> cloud <-> browser/dashboard-initiated command push) as 'DESIGNED, NOT YET BUILT.' apra-fleet-reorg's own envelope/SSE/Postgres relay system (built during the hub-spoke migration sprint, src/services/relay-executor.ts, relay-request.ts, hub-client.ts, spoke.ts) proved the wire-protocol semantics (at-least-once delivery, TTL, redelivery, workspace isolation) but per this epic's scoping decision is not the production transport -- SSH remains the primary/permanent execution transport, and NAT-traversal relay is a secondary transport for machines SSH cannot reach, not a priority for the current bootstrap/sync-focused phase.

Revisit once: (a) fleet-dashboard ships its bidirectional WebSocket half, and (b) there is a concrete customer need for command execution against a machine SSH cannot reach. At that point, decide whether apra-fleet.exe's relay client speaks fleet-dashboard's WebSocket protocol (likely) or something else, using apra-fleet-reorg's already-built envelope semantics as the reference design.
backlogTasks.acceptance_criteria: No action expected until explicitly reprioritized. This issue exists so the deferred decision and its rationale are not lost.
